items.correct: Removing material from a solid block to reveal the form (e.g., carving stone).
items.why: Students confuse 'subtractive' with arithmetic. In sculpture, additive = building up (clay, welding); subtractive = taking away (carving). Michelangelo described 'freeing' figures from the marble - quintessential subtractive thinking.

items.correct: Strong contrast between light and dark to model three-dimensional form.
items.why: Students think it's about subject matter (night scenes). Chiaroscuro is a TECHNIQUE using value contrast to suggest volume - exemplified by Caravaggio but used since the Renaissance to model form.

items.q: What did Renaissance artists rediscover that medieval art lacked?
items.correct: Linear perspective and naturalistic representation of the human body.
items.why: Students assume medieval art was colourless. It was vivid but flat - the Renaissance breakthrough was mathematical PERSPECTIVE (Brunelleschi) and anatomical realism, not the introduction of colour.

items.q: Carving is which type of process?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Subtractive. “Additive” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Reclining figures. “Pop art prints” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Year of Tracey Emin's 'My Bed' installation?
items.why: Exact answer: 1998. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
